facts.text: Planet Earth (2006) took over five years to film and was shot in 204 locations across 62 countries.
David Attenborough narrated his first nature documentary, Zoo Quest, in 1954.
The word 'documentary' was first used by Scottish filmmaker John Grierson in 1926 to describe Robert Flaherty's film Moana.
Nanook of the North (1922) by Robert Flaherty is widely considered the first feature-length documentary film.
Ken Burns is known for his signature technique of slowly panning across still photographs, now called the 'Ken Burns effect.'
The Burns effect is so well known that Apple named a feature after it in iMovie and other software.
Michael Moore's Fahrenheit 9/11 (2004) remains the highest-grossing documentary of all time, earning over $222 million worldwide.
Werner Herzog has directed more than 70 films, including both fiction features and documentaries.
Errol Morris invented the Interrotron, a device that allows interview subjects to look directly into the camera while maintaining eye contact with the interviewer.
Frederick Wiseman has made over 40 documentary films, almost all examining American institutions.
Morgan Spurlock's Super Size Me (2004) chronicled his 30-day experiment of eating only McDonald's food.
The Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature has been awarded annually since 1942.
Making a Murderer (2015) became one of Netflix's most-watched original series and sparked widespread public debate about the justice system.
The Jinx: The Life and Deaths of Robert Durst ended with a shocking hot-mic confession that led to Durst's arrest.
Tiger King became the most-watched show on Netflix during the early weeks of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020.
Wild Wild Country (2018) explored the Rajneeshee movement in rural Oregon and won an Emmy for Outstanding Documentary Series.
Blue Planet II (2017) is credited with significantly raising global awareness about plastic pollution in the oceans.
Our Planet (2019), narrated by David Attenborough, was Netflix's first major nature documentary series.
The Last Dance (2020) docuseries about Michael Jordan and the 1997–98 Chicago Bulls drew an average of 5.6 million viewers per episode on ESPN.
Woodstock (1970), the documentary about the legendary 1969 music festival, won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
Amy (2015), about singer Amy Winehouse, won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature and grossed over $23 million.
Montage of Heck (2015) was the first fully authorized documentary about Kurt Cobain and featured previously unseen home movies and recordings.
13th (2016) by Ava DuVernay examines the intersection of race, justice, and mass incarceration in the United States.
Blackfish (2013) investigated the treatment of orcas at SeaWorld and led to a significant decline in the park's attendance and stock price.
An Inconvenient Truth (2006) featuring Al Gore won two Academy Awards and helped bring climate change into mainstream public discourse.
Bowling for Columbine (2002) by Michael Moore won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature and explored gun violence in America.
Free Solo (2018) documented Alex Honnold's free solo climb of El Capitan's 3,000-foot vertical rock face in Yosemite.
Icarus (2017) began as a film about doping in amateur cycling but accidentally uncovered Russia's state-sponsored Olympic doping program.
Hoop Dreams (1994) followed two inner-city Chicago teenagers for over four years as they pursued their basketball dreams.
ESPN's 30 for 30 documentary series has produced over 100 films since its debut in 2009.
The Vietnam War (2017) by Ken Burns and Lynn Novick was an 18-hour, 10-episode documentary series that took over a decade to produce.
They Shall Not Grow Old (2018) by Peter Jackson used cutting-edge technology to colorize and restore World War I footage.
Jiro Dreams of Sushi (2011) profiles 85-year-old sushi master Jiro Ono, whose restaurant has three Michelin stars.
Chef's Table, which debuted on Netflix in 2015, was one of the first original documentary series commissioned by the streaming platform.
Netflix has become the largest commissioner of documentary content in the world, releasing dozens of original docs each year.
HBO Documentary Films has won more Academy Awards for documentary than any other distributor.
Disney+ released the National Geographic-branded documentary Free Solo as one of its launch titles in 2019.
Cosmos: A Personal Voyage (1980) hosted by Carl Sagan was watched by over 500 million people in 60 countries.
Cosmos: A Spacetime Odyssey (2014) was hosted by Neil deGrasse Tyson as a follow-up to Sagan's original series.
March of the Penguins (2005) earned $127 million worldwide, making it one of the highest-grossing nature documentaries ever.
Man on Wire (2008) documented Philippe Petit's 1974 high-wire walk between the Twin Towers of the World Trade Center.
The Thin Blue Line (1988) by Errol Morris helped exonerate Randall Dale Adams, who had been wrongfully convicted of murder.
Grizzly Man (2005) by Werner Herzog told the story of Timothy Treadwell, who lived among grizzly bears in Alaska for 13 summers.
Shoah (1985) by Claude Lanzmann is a nine-and-a-half-hour documentary about the Holocaust, composed entirely of interviews and location footage.
The Act of Killing (2012) had Indonesian death squad leaders reenact their real-life mass killings in the style of their favorite film genres.
Grey Gardens (1975) by the Maysles brothers documented the eccentric lives of Edith Bouvier Beale and her daughter, relatives of Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis.
Searching for Sugar Man (2012) told the remarkable story of Rodriguez, a forgotten American musician who became a huge star in South Africa without knowing it.
The documentary Senna (2010) about Formula One driver Ayrton Senna was composed entirely of archival footage with no talking-head interviews.
Won't You Be My Neighbor? (2018) about Fred Rogers grossed over $22 million, making it the highest-grossing biographical documentary at the time.
RBG (2018) about Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg earned over $14 million at the box office.
Citizenfour (2014) by Laura Poitras documented Edward Snowden's NSA revelations in real time and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
The Cove (2009) exposed the annual dolphin hunt in Taiji, Japan, and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
Inside Job (2010) examined the causes of the 2008 global financial crisis and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
O.J.: Made in America (2016) ran nearly eight hours and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, the longest film ever to win an Oscar.
Exit Through the Gift Shop (2010) by the street artist Banksy blurred the line between documentary and elaborate prank.
Waltz with Bashir (2008) was an animated documentary about the 1982 Lebanon War, one of the first animated films to be nominated for Best Foreign Language Film at the Oscars.
The first documentary to win the Palme d'Or at Cannes was Fahrenheit 9/11 in 2004.
Cinéma vérité, meaning 'truthful cinema,' is a documentary style that emerged in France in the 1960s emphasizing naturalistic observation.
Direct cinema, the American counterpart to cinéma vérité, was pioneered by filmmakers like D.A. Pennebaker and the Maysles brothers.
Don't Look Back (1967) by D.A. Pennebaker followed Bob Dylan on his 1965 concert tour of the United Kingdom.
The opening sequence of Don't Look Back, featuring Dylan flipping cue cards, is one of the most iconic scenes in documentary history.
Gimme Shelter (1970) captured the Rolling Stones' disastrous 1969 concert at Altamont Speedway, including the fatal stabbing of a concertgoer.
Roger & Me (1989) was Michael Moore's debut film, documenting the impact of General Motors closing auto plants in Flint, Michigan.
Michael Moore has directed six of the top 20 highest-grossing documentaries in North American box office history.
Fahrenheit 9/11 earned $119 million domestically, more than many Hollywood blockbusters released the same year.
Werner Herzog once ate his own shoe after losing a bet to Errol Morris, and it was captured in the short film Werner Herzog Eats His Shoe (1980).
Werner Herzog was once shot with an air rifle during an interview with the BBC and calmly continued speaking.
Ken Burns' The Civil War (1990) attracted 40 million viewers when it first aired on PBS, the most-watched program in the network's history.
Ken Burns has spent his entire career making documentaries for PBS, never working for a major Hollywood studio.
The Up series, which began in 1964 with Seven Up!, revisits the same group of British individuals every seven years and has continued for over 60 years.
Spellbound (2002) followed eight teenagers competing in the National Spelling Bee and was nominated for an Academy Award.
An Inconvenient Sequel: Truth to Power (2017) was the follow-up to Al Gore's original climate change documentary.
Supersize Me 2: Holy Chicken! (2019) saw Morgan Spurlock open his own fast-food restaurant to expose industry practices.
The first documentary shown on Netflix streaming was the 2009 film Food, Inc.
Food, Inc. (2008) examined corporate farming in America and was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
Forks Over Knives (2011) advocated for a whole-food, plant-based diet and became one of the most influential food documentaries.
The Game Changers (2018) explored plant-based eating among elite athletes and was executive produced by James Cameron.
David Attenborough has been making nature documentaries for over 70 years, beginning in the 1950s.
Planet Earth II (2016) was the first BBC nature series filmed entirely in Ultra High Definition (4K).
The iguana vs. snakes chase scene from Planet Earth II became a viral sensation, viewed over 100 million times online.
A Life on Our Planet (2020) was described by David Attenborough as his 'witness statement' for the natural world.
Dynasties (2018) narrated by Attenborough followed five endangered species — chimpanzees, emperor penguins, lions, painted wolves, and tigers.
The Hunt (2015) BBC series explored predator-prey relationships and took over three years to film.
Frozen Planet (2011) explored life in the Arctic and Antarctic and attracted over 10 million viewers per episode in the UK.
Seven Worlds, One Planet (2019) dedicated an entire episode to each continent's unique wildlife.
Night on Earth (2020) on Netflix used cutting-edge low-light camera technology to film animals after dark.
The Netflix documentary My Octopus Teacher (2020) won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
My Octopus Teacher followed filmmaker Craig Foster's year-long relationship with a wild octopus in a South African kelp forest.
The Social Dilemma (2020) featured former tech executives warning about the dangers of social media and was watched by over 100 million people on Netflix.
The Great Hack (2019) explored the Cambridge Analytica data scandal and its effects on democracy.
Making a Murderer filmmakers Laura Ricciardi and Moira Demos spent 10 years making the series.
The Staircase (2004–2018) was one of the earliest true crime documentary series, following the Michael Peterson murder case over 16 years.
Paradise Lost: The Child Murders at Robin Hood Hills (1996) helped galvanize the movement to free the West Memphis Three.
The Keepers (2017) investigated the unsolved murder of a nun and allegations of abuse at a Baltimore Catholic school.
Dear Zachary: A Letter to a Son About His Father (2008) is frequently cited as one of the most emotionally devastating documentaries ever made.
Evil Genius (2018) documented the bizarre 'collar bomb' bank robbery case in Erie, Pennsylvania.
Don't F**k with Cats (2019) showed how internet sleuths tracked down a killer who posted animal cruelty videos online.
The Imposter (2012) told the true story of a French con artist who convinced a Texas family he was their missing son.
Abducted in Plain Sight (2017) became a viral sensation on Netflix due to its astonishing true-crime story.
Conversations with a Killer: The Ted Bundy Tapes (2019) used archival interviews with the serial killer recorded before his execution.
The Innocence Files (2020) on Netflix examined cases handled by the Innocence Project, a nonprofit legal organization.
Murdaugh Murders: A Southern Scandal (2023) became one of Netflix's most-watched documentary series.
American Murder: The Family Next Door (2020) about the Chris Watts case was composed entirely of archival footage, social media posts, and law enforcement recordings.
Capturing the Friedmans (2003) forced audiences to question the reliability of memory and testimony in an alleged child abuse case.
The Trials of Gabriel Fernandez (2020) examined the systems that failed an eight-year-old California boy.
Night Stalker: The Hunt for a Serial Killer (2021) on Netflix covered the investigation into Richard Ramirez.
Crime Scene: The Vanishing at the Cecil Hotel (2021) explored the mysterious death of Elisa Lam.
I'll Be Gone in the Dark (2020) on HBO was based on Michelle McNamara's book about the Golden State Killer.
Ken Burns' Baseball (1994) was an 18-and-a-half-hour documentary series that became one of the most comprehensive histories of the sport.
ESPN's 30 for 30 series was created in 2009 to celebrate ESPN's 30th anniversary with 30 documentary films.
The Two Escobars (2010) from 30 for 30 explored the intersection of soccer and drug cartels in Colombia.
Broke (2012) from 30 for 30 examined how professional athletes go bankrupt despite earning millions.
The Best That Never Was (2010) told the story of Marcus Dupree, a high school football prodigy whose career was derailed by injury.
Survive and Advance (2013) from 30 for 30 covered NC State's miraculous 1983 NCAA basketball championship run.
Hoop Dreams was filmed over a four-and-a-half-year period and edited down from over 250 hours of footage.
Free Solo won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature in 2019, with Alex Honnold's climb described as one of the greatest athletic feats ever filmed.
Icarus filmmaker Bryan Fogel initially set out to dope himself for an amateur cycling race before uncovering the Russian scandal.
Icarus won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature in 2018.
Murderball (2005) followed the U.S. Paralympic wheelchair rugby team and was nominated for an Academy Award.
Pumping Iron (1977) helped launch Arnold Schwarzenegger's career by documenting his preparation for the 1975 Mr. Olympia contest.
When We Were Kings (1996) documented the legendary 'Rumble in the Jungle' boxing match between Muhammad Ali and George Foreman.
The Endless Summer (1966) followed two surfers traveling the world in search of the perfect wave and became a cult classic.
Senna (2010) is regularly voted one of the greatest sports documentaries ever made.
Undefeated (2011) followed a high school football team in inner-city Memphis and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
14 Peaks: Nothing Is Impossible (2021) documented Nirmal Purja's attempt to summit all 14 eight-thousander mountains in seven months.
The Dawn Wall (2017) followed Tommy Caldwell and Kevin Jorgeson's historic free climb of the Dawn Wall of El Capitan.
Meru (2015) documented three climbers attempting the nearly impossible Shark's Fin route on Mount Meru in the Himalayas.
The Last Dance drew an average of 5.6 million viewers per episode, making it ESPN's most-watched documentary content ever.
Ken Burns' The War (2007) was a 14-hour series exploring the American experience during World War II.
The Fog of War (2003) by Errol Morris featured former Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara reflecting on the Vietnam War and won the Academy Award.
Restrepo (2010) embedded with a platoon of U.S. soldiers in Afghanistan's Korengal Valley, one of the most dangerous postings of the war.
Sebastian Junger co-directed Restrepo and named it after a medic in the platoon who was killed in action.
Winter on Fire: Ukraine's Fight for Freedom (2015) documented the 2013–2014 Euromaidan protests in Kyiv.
The Vietnam War by Ken Burns and Lynn Novick featured over 100 interviews and took 10 years to produce.
They Shall Not Grow Old used machine learning and lip readers to add audio to silent World War I footage.
Peter Jackson's team colorized and converted over 100 hours of archival footage for They Shall Not Grow Old.
5 Broken Cameras (2011) was filmed by Palestinian farmer Emad Burnat and documented nonviolent resistance in the West Bank.
The Square (2013) covered the Egyptian Revolution of 2011 from the perspective of activists in Tahrir Square.
Cartel Land (2015) paralleled vigilante groups fighting drug cartels on both sides of the U.S.–Mexico border.
Last Days in Vietnam (2014) documented the chaotic final days of the Vietnam War and the evacuations from Saigon.
Hearts and Minds (1974) was one of the most influential anti-Vietnam War documentaries and won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
Jiro Ono was 85 years old at the time Jiro Dreams of Sushi was filmed and had been making sushi for over 75 years.
Chef's Table has profiled chefs from over 30 countries across its multiple seasons.
Cooked (2016), based on Michael Pollan's book, explored the four elements — fire, water, air, and earth — as they relate to cooking.
Salt Fat Acid Heat (2018) was based on Samin Nosrat's bestselling cookbook.
Rotten (2018) on Netflix investigated fraud, corruption, and supply chain issues in the global food industry.
The Mind of a Chef (2012–2017) on PBS was narrated by Anthony Bourdain and explored culinary creativity.
Somm (2012) followed four candidates preparing for the notoriously difficult Master Sommelier exam.
Netflix's Taco Chronicles (2019) dedicated each episode to a different type of taco and its cultural history.
Street Food (2019) on Netflix showcased street food vendors across Asia and Latin America.
Ugly Delicious (2018) hosted by chef David Chang challenged notions of food authenticity and cultural ownership.
The first Oscar for Best Documentary Feature was awarded in 1942 to Churchill's Island, a Canadian war film.
The category of Best Documentary Short Subject was introduced at the Oscars in 1942.
A total of five documentary features have been nominated for the Palme d'Or at Cannes and only one has won.
Bowling for Columbine won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature on March 23, 2003.
An Inconvenient Truth won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature on February 25, 2007.
The Sundance Film Festival is one of the most important launchpads for documentary films in the world.
Sundance's U.S. Documentary Grand Jury Prize has been awarded annually since 1989.
The Toronto International Film Festival (TIFF) has premiered many Oscar-winning documentaries.
Hot Docs in Toronto is the largest documentary film festival in North America.
IDFA (International Documentary Film Festival Amsterdam) is the world's largest documentary film festival.
Tribeca Film Festival, co-founded by Robert De Niro, has a significant documentary section.
The Grierson Trust, named after documentary pioneer John Grierson, gives annual awards for documentaries in the UK.
Chris Marker's La Jetée (1962) was a 28-minute French documentary-style film composed almost entirely of still photographs.
Night and Fog (1956) by Alain Resnais was one of the first documentaries about the Holocaust and remains one of the most powerful.
Koyaanisqatsi (1982) is a documentary with no narration or dialogue, using only time-lapse photography and a Philip Glass score.
The word Koyaanisqatsi comes from the Hopi language and means 'life out of balance.'
Baraka (1992) was filmed in 24 countries on six continents and used no words — only images and music.
Samsara (2011), the spiritual sequel to Baraka, was filmed over five years in 25 countries.
The Corporation (2003) examined the modern corporation as a legal 'person' and diagnosed its behavior as psychopathic.
Particle Fever (2013) documented the search for the Higgs boson at CERN's Large Hadron Collider.
Apollo 11 (2019) used exclusively archival footage, much of it never before seen, to document the first Moon landing.
For All Mankind (1989) compiled NASA footage from all nine crewed Apollo missions into a single narrative.
In the Shadow of the Moon (2007) featured interviews with most of the surviving Apollo astronauts.
Cosmos: A Personal Voyage was partly funded by the German TV network WDR, as Carl Sagan struggled to find American backers.
Cosmos was the most-watched PBS series in history for a decade after its 1980 premiere.
Carl Sagan's catchphrase 'billions and billions' became synonymous with his Cosmos series, though he claimed he never actually said it.
Into the Inferno (2016) by Werner Herzog and volcanologist Clive Oppenheimer explored active volcanoes around the world.
Werner Herzog's Encounters at the End of the World (2007) documented life at the McMurdo Station in Antarctica.
Cave of Forgotten Dreams (2010) by Werner Herzog was one of the first documentaries shot in 3D and explored the Chauvet Cave in France.
Werner Herzog once said, 'The poet must not avert his eyes,' to describe his philosophy of documentary filmmaking.
Frederick Wiseman's Titicut Follies (1967) was the first film to be banned in the United States for reasons other than obscenity or national security.
Wiseman's documentaries have covered institutions ranging from hospitals and schools to zoos and ballet companies.
Errol Morris worked as a private detective before becoming a filmmaker, a skill he applied to investigative documentaries.
The Thin Blue Line (1988) was so effective in proving Randall Dale Adams's innocence that it essentially functioned as a legal defense.
Standard Operating Procedure (2008) by Errol Morris investigated the Abu Ghraib prison abuse scandal.
Tabloid (2010) by Errol Morris told the bizarre true story of Joyce McKinney, a former beauty queen accused of kidnapping a Mormon missionary.
Agnes Varda's The Gleaners and I (2000) explored themes of waste, consumption, and art using one of the first consumer digital cameras.
Varda was one of the most acclaimed female documentary filmmakers in cinema history.
Barbara Kopple won two Academy Awards for Best Documentary Feature — for Harlan County, U.S.A. (1976) and American Dream (1990).
Harlan County, U.S.A. documented a coal miners' strike in Kentucky and is one of the landmark American documentaries.
The Maysles brothers pioneered the direct cinema movement in America with films like Salesman (1968) and Grey Gardens (1975).
Salesman (1968) followed four door-to-door Bible salesmen and is considered a masterpiece of cinéma vérité.
Kirsten Johnson's Cameraperson (2016) used her accumulated footage from decades of documentary work as a memoir in images.
Joshua Oppenheimer received both an Academy Award nomination and a BAFTA for The Act of Killing.
Honeyland (2019) from North Macedonia became the first film to be nominated for both Best Documentary Feature and Best International Feature Film at the Oscars.
The documentary Collective (2019) from Romania was also nominated for both Best Documentary Feature and Best International Feature Film.
Flee (2021) became the first film to be nominated for Best Animated Feature, Best Documentary Feature, and Best International Feature Film at the same time.
The average budget for a feature-length documentary is significantly lower than narrative films, often under $1 million.
Crowdfunding platforms like Kickstarter and Indiegogo have become major funding sources for independent documentaries.
Catfish (2010) gave rise to the popular MTV series and added 'catfishing' to the cultural lexicon.
The September Issue (2009) provided a behind-the-scenes look at the production of Vogue magazine's largest-ever issue.
Bill Cunningham New York (2010) profiled the iconic New York Times street fashion photographer.
McQueen (2018) explored the life and work of fashion designer Alexander McQueen.
Iris (2014) by Albert Maysles was his final film, profiling 93-year-old fashion icon Iris Apfel.
The True Cost (2015) investigated the environmental and human impact of fast fashion around the world.
Minimalism: A Documentary About the Important Things (2015) explored the growing movement toward living with less.
Tiny (2013) documented the tiny house movement and people living in homes smaller than 200 square feet.
Werner Herzog's Lo and Behold, Reveries of the Connected World (2016) explored the history and future of the internet.
The Inventor: Out for Blood in Silicon Valley (2019) on HBO examined the Theranos fraud and Elizabeth Holmes.
WeWork: Or the Making and Breaking of a $47 Billion Unicorn (2021) chronicled the rise and fall of the coworking company.
The Fyre fraud was the subject of two competing documentaries in 2019: Fyre on Netflix and Fyre Fraud on Hulu.
Enron: The Smartest Guys in the Room (2005) detailed the rise and fall of the Enron Corporation.
The documentary Downfall: The Case Against Boeing (2022) examined the 737 MAX crashes and Boeing's corporate failures.
Miss Americana (2020) on Netflix provided a personal look at Taylor Swift's life and political awakening.
Homecoming (2019) on Netflix captured Beyoncé's historic 2018 Coachella performance.
Beyoncé's Homecoming performance at Coachella was the first headlining set by a Black woman at the festival.
Justin Bieber: Never Say Never (2011) grossed over $99 million worldwide, one of the highest-grossing concert films.
This Is It (2009) documented Michael Jackson's rehearsals for his planned comeback concerts and grossed over $261 million worldwide.
Straight Outta Compton (2015), while a narrative film, drew on decades of documentary footage of N.W.A.
The Beatles: Get Back (2021) by Peter Jackson used over 60 hours of unseen footage and 150 hours of unheard audio from 1969.
Get Back showed the Beatles composing and recording songs that would become the Let It Be album in real time.
What Happened, Miss Simone? (2015) explored the life, music, and activism of Nina Simone.
Summer of Soul (2021) by Questlove unearthed footage from the 1969 Harlem Cultural Festival that had been unseen for over 50 years.
Summer of Soul won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature at the 2022 ceremony.
20 Feet from Stardom (2013) highlighted the lives of backup singers behind major music stars and won the Academy Award.
Searching for Sugar Man won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature at the 2013 ceremony.
The documentary Gimme Danger (2016) by Jim Jarmusch chronicled the career of Iggy Pop and the Stooges.
Kurt Cobain: Montage of Heck was the first documentary authorized by Cobain's family and featured his personal journals and artwork.
Sound City (2013) directed by Dave Grohl explored the legendary recording studio where Nirvana, Tom Petty, and Fleetwood Mac recorded.
Tupac: Resurrection (2003) was narrated entirely by Tupac Shakur using archival audio recordings.
I Am Not Your Negro (2016) was based on James Baldwin's unfinished manuscript and narrated by Samuel L. Jackson.
13th by Ava DuVernay was the first documentary to open the New York Film Festival in its 54-year history.
The 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which abolished slavery, included a clause allowing involuntary servitude as punishment for crime — a key focus of the film 13th.
Strong Island (2017) explored the murder of filmmaker Yance Ford's brother and systemic racism in the justice system.
Time (2020) by Garrett Bradley followed a woman fighting for her husband's release from a 60-year prison sentence.
Crip Camp (2020) traced the disability rights movement back to a revolutionary summer camp in the 1970s.
Blackfish was partially credited with SeaWorld ending its orca breeding program in 2016.
Before the Flood (2016) followed Leonardo DiCaprio as he explored the impacts of climate change around the world.
Chasing Ice (2012) used time-lapse cameras to document the retreat of glaciers due to climate change.
Chasing Coral (2017) documented the bleaching and death of coral reefs worldwide due to rising ocean temperatures.
Racing Extinction (2015) used covert tactics to expose the illegal wildlife trade and species extinction crisis.
The Ivory Game (2016) went undercover to reveal the global ivory trafficking network.
Kiss the Ground (2020) narrated by Woody Harrelson explored regenerative agriculture as a solution to climate change.
David Attenborough's A Life on Our Planet was released in cinemas, on Netflix, and as a companion book simultaneously.
Seaspiracy (2021) on Netflix controversially argued that commercial fishing is the biggest threat to ocean ecosystems.
Cowspiracy (2014) examined the environmental impact of animal agriculture and was executive produced by Leonardo DiCaprio.
Dominion (2018) used drone and hidden camera footage to expose conditions in Australian animal farming.
Virunga (2014) documented the fight to protect the endangered mountain gorillas in the Congo's Virunga National Park.
Trophy (2017) examined the complex debate around big-game hunting and wildlife conservation in Africa.
The Biggest Little Farm (2018) documented a couple's eight-year effort to build a sustainable farm outside Los Angeles.
The Netflix model of acquiring and distributing documentaries has fundamentally changed the economics of documentary filmmaking.
Hulu, Amazon Prime Video, and Apple TV+ have all significantly invested in original documentary content since 2018.
Apple TV+'s first documentary series, Home (2020), explored innovative architecture around the world.
Disney+'s partnership with National Geographic brought landmark nature documentaries to the streaming platform.
HBO Max (now Max) has continued HBO's legacy of prestigious documentary programming.
Discovery+ launched in 2021 as a streaming service dedicated largely to unscripted and documentary content.
The Paramount+ documentary series The Real Criminal Minds examined true crime cases.
Peacock's documentary lineup includes true crime, sports, and social issue films.
YouTube has become a major platform for independent documentary filmmakers to share their work.
The rise of short-form documentaries on YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram has created new audiences for nonfiction storytelling.
Vice Media built much of its early reputation on short-form documentary journalism.
The cost of high-quality camera equipment has dropped dramatically, democratizing documentary filmmaking.
Drone cinematography has revolutionized the way nature and landscape documentaries are shot.
GoPro cameras have enabled documentary filmmakers to capture extreme sports and adventure footage in ways previously impossible.
The use of thermal imaging and night-vision cameras has opened up new possibilities for wildlife documentary filmmaking.
IMAX documentaries like Deep Sea 3D and Hubble 3D have brought immersive nonfiction storytelling to giant screens.
Hidden camera and undercover filming techniques have been crucial in investigative documentaries exposing abuses.
Documentary podcasts like Serial (2014) have blurred the line between audio and visual documentary storytelling.
Serial, while an audio podcast, led directly to the documentary series The Case Against Adnan Syed (2019) on HBO.
The use of reenactments in documentaries is controversial — some purists consider it a violation of the genre's principles.
Errol Morris has defended the use of reenactments, arguing they can reveal truth in ways observational footage cannot.
The 'fly-on-the-wall' style of documentary aims to observe subjects without interference from the filmmaker.
Participatory documentaries feature the filmmaker as an active character, as seen in Morgan Spurlock's and Michael Moore's work.
Reflexive documentaries acknowledge the filmmaking process itself and question the nature of representation.
Performative documentaries emphasize subjective experience and emotional engagement over objective presentation.
Poetic documentaries prioritize mood, tone, and visual associations over conventional narrative structure.
Expository documentaries use voice-of-God narration to directly address the viewer — the most traditional documentary mode.
Bill Nichols defined six modes of documentary filmmaking: poetic, expository, observational, participatory, reflexive, and performative.
The verité style of documentary was heavily influenced by the development of portable sync-sound cameras in the 1960s.
Nanook of the North was controversial because director Flaherty staged many of the scenes he presented as real life.
The ethical debate surrounding reenactments and staging in documentaries has persisted since the genre's earliest days.
Triumph of the Will (1935) by Leni Riefenstahl remains one of the most technically accomplished yet morally troubling documentaries ever made.
Why We Fight (1943–1945) was a series of seven propaganda films commissioned by the U.S. government and directed by Frank Capra.
The Battle of Midway (1942), directed by John Ford, was filmed during the actual battle and won an Academy Award.
John Huston directed three World War II documentaries for the U.S. Army, including The Battle of San Pietro (1945).
Let There Be Light (1946) by John Huston documented PTSD in returning soldiers and was suppressed by the U.S. Army for over 30 years.
The World at War (1973–1974) is a 26-episode British documentary series widely regarded as the definitive TV history of World War II.
Ken Burns' The Roosevelts: An Intimate History (2014) explored the lives of Theodore, Franklin, and Eleanor Roosevelt over 14 hours.
Ken Burns' Country Music (2019) was an eight-part, 16-hour documentary that traced the genre's entire history.
Ken Burns' Jazz (2001) was a 10-part series exploring the history of jazz music in America.
Ken Burns' The National Parks: America's Best Idea (2009) led to increased visitation at national parks.
Eyes on the Prize (1987, 1990) is the most acclaimed documentary series about the American civil rights movement.
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed into law on July 2, 1964, an event extensively covered in Eyes on the Prize.
Paris Is Burning (1990) documented ball culture in New York City and introduced mainstream audiences to voguing.
The Times of Harvey Milk (1984) won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
How to Survive a Plague (2012) documented the activism of ACT UP and TAG in fighting the AIDS epidemic.
United States vs. Billie Holiday and other narrative films drew heavily on documentary research about the War on Drugs.
The Oscar-winning documentary Born into Brothels (2004) gave cameras to children of sex workers in Calcutta.
Life Itself (2014) was a documentary about legendary film critic Roger Ebert, completed shortly after his death.
Visions of Light (1992) celebrated the art of cinematography through interviews with legendary directors of photography.
The Story of Film: An Odyssey (2011) was a 15-hour documentary series tracing the complete history of world cinema.
Side by Side (2012) hosted by Keanu Reeves explored the transition from photochemical film to digital cinema.
Room 237 (2012) examined various conspiracy theories and hidden meanings in Stanley Kubrick's The Shining.
Jodorowsky's Dune (2013) documented Alejandro Jodorowsky's ambitious but unrealized adaptation of Dune.
Lost in La Mancha (2002) documented Terry Gilliam's failed attempt to film The Man Who Killed Don Quixote.
Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse (1991) documented the troubled production of Apocalypse Now.
Burden of Dreams (1982) by Les Blank documented the extreme difficulties of Werner Herzog filming Fitzcarraldo in the Amazon.
ILM: Industrial Light & Magic (2022) on Disney+ celebrated the special effects company founded by George Lucas.
The First Movie (2009) by Mark Cousins explored how children in a Kurdish village respond to cinema.
Roadrunner: A Film About Anthony Bourdain (2021) controversially used AI to recreate Bourdain's voice.
The use of AI-generated voiceovers in Roadrunner sparked a debate about ethics in documentary filmmaking.
Deepfake technology presents new ethical challenges for the documentary genre.
Nanook of the North was released on June 11, 1922, and is over 100 years old.
Robert Flaherty's Man of Aran (1934) documented life on the Aran Islands off the west coast of Ireland.
Dziga Vertov's Man with a Movie Camera (1929) was a groundbreaking Soviet documentary about life in Ukrainian and Russian cities.
Man with a Movie Camera is frequently ranked as one of the greatest documentaries ever made.
Olympia (1938) by Leni Riefenstahl documented the 1936 Berlin Olympics and introduced many techniques still used in sports broadcasting.
Primary (1960) by Robert Drew followed John F. Kennedy and Hubert Humphrey during the 1960 Wisconsin primary election.
Primary is considered one of the first films of the American direct cinema movement.
Chronicle of a Summer (1961) by Jean Rouch and Edgar Morin is considered the founding film of cinéma vérité.
The Sorrow and the Pity (1969) challenged the myth of widespread French resistance during World War II.
Marcel Ophüls' The Sorrow and the Pity was banned from French television for over a decade after its release.
Crumb (1994) by Terry Zwigoff profiled underground comics artist Robert Crumb and his eccentric family.
American Movie (1999) followed aspiring filmmaker Mark Borchardt's efforts to complete his ultra-low-budget horror film.
Dark Days (2000) by Marc Singer documented the lives of people living in the Freedom Tunnel beneath New York City's Riverside Park.
Stevie (2002) by Steve James (who also directed Hoop Dreams) was a deeply personal study of a troubled young man in rural Illinois.
The Gleaners and I was shot by Agnes Varda using a handheld digital camera, helping legitimize consumer-grade cameras for documentary use.
Touching the Void (2003) used dramatic reenactments to tell the harrowing climbing story of Joe Simpson and Simon Yates.
Deep Water (2006) documented the 1968 Golden Globe yacht race and Donald Crowhurst's tragic and mysterious journey.
Man on Wire won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature at the 2009 ceremony.
Project Nim (2011) by James Marsh (who directed Man on Wire) told the story of a chimpanzee raised as a human.
Blackfish director Gabriela Cowperthwaite was inspired to make the film after the death of SeaWorld trainer Dawn Brancheau.
The Look of Silence (2014), Joshua Oppenheimer's companion film to The Act of Killing, followed a victim's family confronting perpetrators.
Tower (2016) used rotoscope animation to recreate the 1966 University of Texas mass shooting.
Kate Plays Christine (2016) blurred the line between documentary and fiction by following an actress preparing to play a role.
Shirkers (2018) explored a stolen film and lost friendship in a highly personal and genre-bending way.
Minding the Gap (2018) by Bing Liu used skateboarding to explore themes of domestic abuse, race, and class.
The Cave (2019) documented a female doctor running an underground hospital in war-torn Syria.
American Factory (2019) was the first film released by Barack and Michelle Obama's production company Higher Ground.
American Factory won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature at the 2020 ceremony.
Dick Johnson Is Dead (2020) by Kirsten Johnson used staged death scenes to explore her father's dementia.
Collective (2019) followed journalists investigating a Romanian hospital corruption scandal after a nightclub fire killed 64 people.
Fire of Love (2022) chronicled the lives and eventual deaths of French volcanologists Katia and Maurice Krafft.
All the Beauty and the Bloodshed (2022) by Laura Poitras won the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival, a rare honor for a documentary.
Navalny (2022) documented Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny's investigation into his own poisoning and won the Academy Award.
20 Days in Mariupol (2023) won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature and documented the Russian siege of Mariupol.
Prehistoric Planet (2022) on Apple TV+ used CGI to recreate dinosaurs in a nature documentary style narrated by David Attenborough.
The Alpinist (2021) documented the life and climbs of Marc-André Leclerc, one of the most daring free soloists in history.
Return to Space (2022) on Netflix followed Elon Musk and SpaceX's mission to return American astronauts to the International Space Station.
The Rescue (2021) by the Free Solo directors documented the 2018 Tham Luang cave rescue in Thailand.
The Rescue used a combination of reenactments, archival footage, and interviews to tell the cave rescue story.
Stutz (2022) directed by Jonah Hill featured conversations with his therapist in a unique and personal documentary.
Pamela, A Love Story (2023) on Netflix was an authorized documentary about Pamela Anderson's life.
Arnold (2023) on Netflix was a three-part docuseries about Arnold Schwarzenegger's life and career.
Beckham (2023) on Netflix became one of the most-watched documentary series in the platform's history.
The Tinder Swindler (2022) on Netflix exposed a con man who defrauded women he met on Tinder out of millions.
Bad Vegan (2022) on Netflix told the story of a New York restaurateur who was manipulated into fraud.
The Puppet Master: Hunting the Ultimate Con Man (2022) on Netflix exposed one of the UK's most notorious fraudsters.
Keep Sweet: Pray and Obey (2022) on Netflix documented the abuses within the FLDS church under Warren Jeffs.
Our Father (2022) on Netflix uncovered the story of a fertility doctor who secretly fathered dozens of children.
Girl in the Picture (2022) on Netflix unraveled a complex true crime mystery of identity and coercion.
The first documentary to gross over $100 million at the worldwide box office was Fahrenheit 9/11 in 2004.
March of the Penguins was narrated by Morgan Freeman in the English version, while the French original had voiced penguin characters.
Jackass: The Movie (2002), while classified as comedy, contained elements of documentary filmmaking.
Borat (2006) blended scripted comedy with genuine documentarian interactions, challenging genre boundaries.
Bruno (2009), also by Sacha Baron Cohen, continued the mockumentary-documentary hybrid format.
This Is Spinal Tap (1984) popularized the mockumentary format, though it is not a true documentary.
Best in Show (2000) and other Christopher Guest films have perfected the mockumentary genre.
The Blair Witch Project (1999) used found-footage documentary techniques to create a horror film phenomenon.
Lake Mungo (2008) was an Australian horror film presented entirely as a mockumentary.
The Office (2001–2003, UK; 2005–2013, US) popularized the mockumentary format for television comedy.
Modern Family (2009–2020) also employed the mockumentary format popularized by The Office.
Documentary filmmaking was originally done on film, but the transition to digital video in the 2000s made production far more accessible.
The ARRI Alexa and RED camera systems are among the most popular high-end cameras used for modern documentary filmmaking.
Many wildlife documentaries take years to film because they must wait for specific animal behaviors to occur naturally.
Planet Earth reportedly took 2,000 days of filming across five years to complete.
The BBC Natural History Unit, based in Bristol, England, is the world's largest wildlife documentary production unit.
National Geographic has been producing documentary content since the 1960s under its television division.
The Discovery Channel launched in 1985 and became one of the first cable channels dedicated to documentary programming.
PBS's Frontline series has been producing investigative documentaries since 1983 and has won numerous Peabody and Emmy Awards.
POV on PBS has been showcasing independent documentaries since 1988 and is the longest-running documentary series on television.
The Criterion Collection has released numerous restored documentaries, helping preserve the genre's history.
The Peabody Award, one of the most prestigious in electronic media, regularly honors outstanding documentaries.
The Cinema Eye Honors, founded in 2008, are dedicated exclusively to recognizing nonfiction filmmaking.
The IDA (International Documentary Association) Documentary Awards have honored the best in nonfiction filmmaking since 1984.
The True/False Film Fest in Columbia, Missouri, has become one of the most respected documentary festivals in the United States.
Full Frame Documentary Film Festival in Durham, North Carolina, is another prestigious American documentary festival.
Sheffield DocFest in England is one of the most important documentary festivals in Europe.
CPH:DOX in Copenhagen has grown into one of the largest documentary film festivals in the world.
Sundance premiered both Blackfish and Searching for Sugar Man, which went on to win Academy Awards.
The Sundance Documentary Fund provides crucial grants to documentary filmmakers around the world.
Netflix's success with documentaries has led every major streaming platform to invest more heavily in nonfiction content.
Streaming platforms now acquire many documentaries directly from film festivals, sometimes before theatrical release.
The pandemic accelerated the direct-to-streaming release model for documentaries.
Tiger King was released on Netflix on March 20, 2020, just as COVID-19 lockdowns began across the United States.
Tiger King was viewed by 64 million households in its first four weeks on Netflix.
The documentary series Wild Wild Country was based on archival footage the Rajneeshees had filmed themselves.
Wild Wild Country won the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Documentary or Nonfiction Series in 2018.
The Defiant Ones (2017) on HBO documented the partnership between Jimmy Iovine and Dr. Dre.
Hip-Hop Evolution (2016–2020) on Netflix traced the history of hip-hop music from its origins to the present day.
Quincy (2018) on Netflix profiled the legendary music producer Quincy Jones and was co-directed by his daughter Rashida Jones.
Springsteen on Broadway (2018) on Netflix captured Bruce Springsteen's one-man show on Broadway.
Taylor Swift: The Eras Tour (2023) was technically a concert film but used documentary elements.
The documentary Stop Making Sense (1984) about the Talking Heads is widely considered the greatest concert film ever made.
Stop Making Sense was re-released in IMAX in 2023 and again became a theatrical hit.
The Last Waltz (1978) by Martin Scorsese documented The Band's final concert.
Shut Up and Play the Hits (2012) captured LCD Soundsystem's final concert at Madison Square Garden.
David Attenborough was born on May 8, 1926, and has been making documentaries for over seven decades.
Blue Planet (2001) was the first nature series to extensively explore Earth's marine environments.
Green Planet (2022) explored the hidden world of plants using revolutionary time-lapse and macro photography.
The Hunt for the Wilderpeople (2016), while a narrative film, was inspired by New Zealand's documentary-rich filmmaking culture.
Jane (2017) used previously unseen footage from the 1960s to tell the story of Jane Goodall's early work with chimpanzees.
Jane Goodall began her research on chimpanzees in Tanzania's Gombe Stream on July 14, 1960.
The Elephant Queen (2019) followed a herd of elephants in Africa and was one of the first documentaries on Apple TV+.
David Attenborough's Blue Planet II episode on the deep sea was filmed at depths of over 1,000 meters.
Werner Herzog believes in the 'ecstatic truth' — that documentaries should capture a deeper truth beyond mere facts.
The documentary format has expanded into virtual reality, with projects like Notes on Blindness VR.
Interactive documentaries allow viewers to choose their own paths through a story, creating personalized experiences.
The MIT Open Documentary Lab studies new forms of documentary storytelling including VR and interactive media.
Documentary films have increasingly been used as evidence in court cases and Congressional hearings.
The documentary Fed Up (2014) narrated by Katie Couric investigated the role of sugar in the American obesity epidemic.
That Sugar Film (2014) was an Australian documentary where filmmaker Damon Gameau consumed a high-sugar diet for 60 days.
The Magic Pill (2017) explored the ketogenic diet and its potential health benefits.
Fasting (2017) examined intermittent fasting through a documentary lens.
What the Health (2017) examined the connection between diet and disease.
Game Changers producer James Cameron owns a plant-based protein company, leading some critics to question the film's objectivity.
Wasted! The Story of Food Waste (2017) narrated by Anthony Bourdain explored global food waste issues.
The Oscar for Best Documentary Feature in 2024 went to 20 Days in Mariupol by Mstyslav Chernov.
Every year, the Academy shortlists around 15 documentary features before narrowing to five nominees.
Non-fiction filmmaking has existed since the very earliest days of cinema — the Lumière brothers' first films in 1895 were essentially documentaries.
The Lumière brothers' Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory (1895) is often considered the first documentary film.
Robert Flaherty is often called the 'father of documentary film' for his pioneering work on Nanook of the North.
John Grierson coined the term 'documentary' and founded the British Documentary Film Movement in the 1930s.
The GPO Film Unit in Britain, led by Grierson, produced innovative documentaries including Night Mail (1936).
Night Mail (1936) featured poetry by W.H. Auden and music by Benjamin Britten.
Humphrey Jennings' Listen to Britain (1942) used sound and image to paint a portrait of wartime Britain.
Point of Order (1964) by Emile de Antonio used only archival footage to document the Army–McCarthy hearings.
Monterey Pop (1968) by D.A. Pennebaker captured the groundbreaking 1967 Monterey International Pop Festival.
Harlan County, U.S.A. won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature in 1977.
Roger & Me was controversial because Michael Moore rearranged the timeline of events for dramatic effect.
Paris Is Burning was released in 1990 and has since become a cultural touchstone for LGBTQ+ communities.
When We Were Kings won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature nearly 22 years after the fight it documented.
Super Size Me grossed over $22 million against a production budget of just $65,000.
The March of the Penguins was released on June 26, 2005, and became a surprise box office hit.
The documentary RBG was released on May 4, 2018.
Won't You Be My Neighbor? was released on June 8, 2018, and became the year's highest-grossing documentary.
My Octopus Teacher won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature on April 25, 2021.
Ken Burns' The Civil War first aired on PBS on September 23, 1990.
Cosmos: A Personal Voyage premiered on September 28, 1980.
